actions.thought: Holding Ten-Deuce offsuit on the Button in a 3-handed game, this hand is far too weak to open even from the best position. It ranks among the bottom tier of starting hands, and there is no reason to risk chips this early in the tournament with such a low-equity hand.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind with A4o. This is a decent hand heads-up against the big blind. An open raise to 3x (300) will often win the blinds outright and gives me the betting initiative if the big blind decides to call. A4o blocks strong Aces and has reasonable equity.

I am defending my Big Blind with J8s, which is a decent hand to play against a small blind open. Given the pot odds of 2:1 and the fact that I will have position post-flop, calling is the optimal strategic play to realize my equity without overcommitting early in the tournament.

The 9h-8d-Td flop is extremely connected and heavily favors the pre-flop caller's range. The big blind can have many combinations of straights, two pairs, sets, pair+straight draws, and flush draws. With just Ace high, no pair, and only a backdoor diamond draw, my hand has very little equity and very poor fold equity against the BB's range. Checking is the most standard and safe play here, looking to give up unless the action checks down favorably.

The board (9h 8d Td) is highly coordinated and dangerous, presenting numerous straight and flush draws. My pair of 8s is weak on this texture and has low equity against many calling ranges if I were to bet. Checking behind allows me to keep the pot small, realize my equity for free, and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn pairs the Ten, which doesn't improve my hand. The board remains extremely dangerous and connected (9h-8d-Td-Th). The opponent's range is full of hands that connect well here, including a Ten, straights, and combo draws. Since the flop checked through, I might still have showdown value with Ace-high against missed draws, but there is no merit to bluffing this board with my specific hand. Checking gives me a free look at the river or an easy fold if my opponent decides to bet.

The board (9h 8d Td Th) is highly coordinated, with straight and flush possibilities everywhere. My pair of eights is extremely vulnerable, and betting here would likely only be called by hands that have me beat or by draws with better equity than mine. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, maintain the flexibility to fold to a potential river bet, and keep the pot size manageable while getting to see a river card for free.

The river is the 2 of hearts, completing a backdoor flush draw. After both the flop and turn checked through, it's very possible my opponent is holding a missed straight or flush draw, or perhaps a weak pair. With Ace high, I have some showdown value and can beat King-high or Queen-high missed draws (like KQ, KJ, QJ, etc.). Betting as a bluff has little merit since my opponent is likely to call with any pair and fold out the hands I already beat. Checking is the best move to realize my showdown value or easily fold if they decide to bet.

The board is extremely coordinated, offering several straight and flush possibilities that have now missed. My pair of eights is weak and primarily serves as a bluff catcher. Since the opponent checked to me, betting is unlikely to get value from worse hands and would only serve to inflate a pot against a range that could contain better pairs or trapped hands. Checking behind is the optimal play to realize my showdown value and minimize risk.
K5 suited is a strong enough hand to open from the button in a 3-handed game. I'll raise to 2.5x to pressure the blinds, steal the pot pre-flop, or play a suited King in position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with a very weak hand, 3-5 offsuit. Playing this hand out of position against a button raise is negative expected value. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have a premium pocket pair (Tens) in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro) raised to 2.5bb, which is a wide opening range. Tens are too strong to just call; I should 3-bet for value and to gain initiative in the hand. A standard 3-bet size from the Big Blind against a Button open is approximately 3.5x to 4x the initial raise. I will raise to 850 to build the pot and potentially end the hand immediately or see a flop with a range advantage.

K5 suited is an acceptable open from the button in a 3-handed game, but faces a sizable 3-bet from the Big Blind. Calling here with a weak kicker is a losing play since we will often be dominated by better Kings, and 4-bet bluffing with this specific holding isn't optimal. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve the stack.

I am on the button, which is the most advantageous position in a three-handed game. My hand, Queen-Nine offsuit, is playable enough to open from this position to apply pressure to the blinds. By raising to 2.5x the big blind, I aim to either win the blinds uncontested or play the hand in position against the small or big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six of clubs. Flashlight opened from the Button to 250. This hand is strong enough to play, but calling from the Small Blind often leads to difficult post-flop situations out of position, especially with a strong player like Pro in the Big Blind. To take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or isolate Flashlight, I will 3-bet to 950. This sizing exerts pressure and builds a pot with a hand that has good nut-flush potential.

Holding Q5 offsuit in the Big Blind facing a button open and a 3-bet from the Small Blind is a trivial fold. My hand has very little equity against their ranges, and investing 850 chips here would be a huge mistake.

I opened with Queen-Nine offsuit from the button, which is a standard steal attempt. However, facing a 3-bet to 950 from the small blind indicates strength. Given that Queen-Nine offsuit has poor equity against a 3-betting range and I would be playing a bloated pot in a disadvantageous spot, the most disciplined play is to fold. I will preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am holding 8-4 offsuit on the button. While the button is the best position to steal blinds, 8-4o is significantly below the threshold for a standard opening range. Given the early stage of the tournament and the fact that both opponents have shown some willingness to contest pots in previous hands, I will play conservatively and fold this weak hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 2h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Even though it's only 50 chips to call, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Playing 3-handed, JTo on the Button is a standard open-raise. The blinds have shown some 3-betting aggression in the past few hands, but folding a playable broadway hand in position is too tight. I will open to 2.5x to try and steal the blinds or play a post-flop pot in position.

Ace-Ten offsuit is a strong hand that performs well against a typical button opening range. Given that I am in the small blind and out of position, I prefer to 3-bet to take the initiative and maximize fold equity, rather than flat-calling and playing a potentially multi-way pot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 8h. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) 3-bet to 900. 3h 8h is a very weak hand that is heavily dominated by the 3-betting ranges of both opponents. Calling 800 more chips into a potential multi-way pot with such low equity is a losing play. Folding is the only sensible option here to preserve my stack.

Opponent Flashlight 3-bet from the Small Blind to 900 after my 2.5x open. While we have position, Jack-Ten offsuit is easily dominated by the 3-betting range, including bigger broadways and big pairs. Calling here represents nearly 10% of my stack with a marginal holding that carries severe reverse implied odds. Folding is the optimal and standard play against a 3-bet with this hand.

In a 3-handed game, position is extremely valuable and my opening range on the button should be wide to steal the blinds. While 4c-Tc suited is at the lower end of my playable range, it is still a suited connector that can make strong hands. I will open the pot to 220, which is 2.2 times the big blind, to apply pressure to the small blind and big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o). Flashlight has opened for 2.2x from the button. While A3o has some high-card value in a three-handed game, it plays very poorly out of position and is easily dominated by the button's opening range (which includes better Aces) or the Big Blind's defending range. Calling would likely lead to a difficult post-flop scenario or invite a squeeze from Pro in the Big Blind. Given the deep stacks and my position, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid losing chips in a marginal spot.

AJo is a strong hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. While a 3-bet is an option, calling keeps the pot manageable out of position and prevents us from being blown off our equity by a 4-bet. We are getting excellent pot odds (over 3:1) to see the flop.

Out of position on a highly coordinated, paired board (Kd Qc Qh) against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard action is to check. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and an Ace overcard, but leading into the Button on a board that heavily favors their opening range would be reckless.

The board texture of Kd-Qc-Qh is extremely favorable to the big blind's defending range, which is likely to contain many Kings and Queens. Holding 10-high with no significant draws or equity, betting into this board is risky as it will likely only be called or raised by hands that have me crushed. Checking behind allows me to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn without committing further chips with air.

The turn gives me the broadway straight with Ad Jh on a Kd Qc Qh Th board. Since the flop checked through, I want to start building the pot and extracting value from hands like a King, a Ten, or various draws. Leading out for around half the pot is a good size to get called by marginal made hands while growing the pot for a river value bet.

The board texture is highly coordinated with Kd-Qc-Qh-Th. My pair of Tens with a 4 kicker is extremely vulnerable, especially since any Jack provides a straight. The opponent's decision to lead out on the turn after checking the flop suggests they have likely connected with the board, either by hitting a straight, a King, a Queen, or a better Ten. Given the significant number of better hands they could be representing, calling with such a weak holding is not a profitable long-term strategy.

Holding Ace-Ten offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game is a standard opening hand. I am first to act and want to exert pressure on the blinds while taking advantage of my position. I will raise to 250, which is a consistent sizing for an opening bet in this structure.
J5o from the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button is a very weak hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Holding KQo in the Big Blind against a button raise is a strong defending hand. With pot odds of 150 to win 400, calling is mathematically sound and allows me to see a flop in a controlled pot. Since the opponent, Flash, has been active with raising, calling is a safer way to realize my equity compared to a 3-bet which might induce a difficult 4-bet scenario.

I am out of position against the button aggressor on an Ace-high board. My King-high hand has little equity against a typical calling or raising range, as the Ace on the board connects well with the opponent's opening range. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and re-evaluate based on the opponent's bet, as leading out would turn a hand with marginal value into a bluff with low fold equity.

I have top pair with a decent kicker on a board that has several drawing possibilities, including diamonds and straight draws. Flashlight checked to me, and as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continuation bet for value and to protect my hand. A bet of 250 into a pot of 550 is a standard sizing that puts pressure on draws and weaker hands while maintaining pot control.

The board texture is Ace-high (Ad-9d-8c) with a flush draw, which is a standard continuation bet for an aggressive button aggressor. My hand (Kh-Qd) has no pair but two overcards to the 8 and 9, and some backdoor potential. Given the pot odds (calling 250 to win a 800 pot), calling is a mathematically sound decision to defend my big blind and re-evaluate on the turn, rather than folding to what could be a speculative c-bet from an active opponent.

The board is Ad-9d-8c-3h. I hold Kh-Qd. After checking on the turn, I am waiting to see how my opponent reacts. Given my lack of a pair and the strength of an Ace-high board, my best strategy is to check-fold or check-call depending on the size of any potential bet from Flash, rather than betting into a board that strongly hits his range.

I have top pair with a ten kicker on a relatively static turn card. The 3h doesn't change the board texture significantly. My opponent checked again, which often indicates a marginal made hand like a weaker Ace or a draw. I want to continue betting for value and to charge any diamond or straight draws. A bet of 550 into a 1050 pot is a standard sizing to build the pot while still being able to fold if I face significant aggression on a dangerous river.

I am facing a bet of 550 into a 1050 pot on an Ace-high board with [8c, Ad, 9d, 3h]. I hold [Qd, Kh], giving me no pair and virtually no showdown equity against the opponent's betting range, which is heavily weighted toward Aces or stronger pocket pairs. My pot odds (calling 550 to win 1600) require ~34% equity, which I do not possess with only 6 outs (3 Kings, 3 Queens) and no guarantee that hitting those outs will win the hand. Folding is the correct, disciplined decision to preserve my stack.

I am on the Button with 9c 6h (Nine of Clubs, Six of Hearts). This is an offsuit, disconnected, weak hand. With two players yet to act in the blinds, it's not profitable to play this hand. Folding is the standard and correct play.

With the button folding, I am in a heads-up situation from the small blind against the big blind. T6s is a respectable, playable hand in this scenario. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to represent strength, aiming to take down the blinds immediately or define the hand in position, which is a standard strategic move in a blind battle.

In Hand #8, I am in the Big Blind holding King-Seven offsuit. The action folded to Flashlight in the Small Blind, who raised to 300. In a blind-vs-blind scenario, ranges are typically wider, and K7o is a strong enough hand to defend. Importantly, as the Big Blind, I will have the advantage of being in position for all subsequent betting rounds against the Small Blind. The pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400) make this a standard call to see a flop.

I have a flush draw with the Ten and Six of diamonds, plus a gutshot straight draw on a 5s, Jd, 7d board. This is a prime candidate for a semi-bluff. By betting now, I can increase the pot size, gain fold equity, and maintain the initiative I established pre-flop. A bet of 350 into the 600 pot puts pressure on my opponent while giving me excellent potential to improve on the turn or river.

I have middle pair (sevens) with a King kicker on a J-7-5 board with a flush draw. In a blind-vs-blind battle, the Small Blind's leading range is wide, and this is a standard continuation bet. My hand is too strong to fold but doesn't warrant a raise yet, as raising might isolate me against better hands like Jacks or overpairs. Calling allows me to utilize my position on the turn and keep their bluffs in the hand while controlling the pot size.

I have successfully hit my flush on the turn with the Ten of Diamonds. The pot is currently 1300. Given that I have a strong made hand, I need to extract value from my opponent who has called previous bets and may be holding a pair, a set, or a straight draw. A bet of 800 represents a strong value bet, roughly 60% of the pot, designed to get called by second-best hands while still building the pot for the river.

On the turn, the board has become significantly more dangerous with the arrival of the Queen of Diamonds. This card completes potential flushes and also puts an overcard to my pair of sevens on the board. Although I hold the King of Diamonds, which gives me the second-best flush draw and a blocker, Flashlight's bet of 800 into a 1300 pot (about 60% pot) represents considerable strength. My pair of sevens has plummeted in value, now being at best fourth pair. While I have about a 19.5% chance to hit a King-high flush on the river, the pot odds (requiring 27.5% equity) don't justify a call based on the draw alone, especially since the Ace of Diamonds could be in Flashlight's range, leaving me drawing dead to a higher flush. Given the tournament context and the strength shown by the Small Blind, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I am in the button position with a very weak hand (7-4 offsuit). There is no incentive to open-raise with such a marginal hand, and since there has been no action before me, the best strategic move is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. Flashlight has folded from the Button, leaving only me and Pro (Big Blind) in the hand. Jack-Four offsuit is a very weak starting hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. While I have shown aggression in previous hands, there is little merit in trying to steal the blinds or play a bloated pot with this hand against an opponent who has shown disciplined folding and calculated play so far. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack.

King-Seven suited is a strong hand to open from the button in a three-handed game. It has good equity and playability post-flop. I will raise to 250 to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position against the small or big blind.

I am in the Small Blind facing a button raise from Flash. My hand, Queen-Five offsuit, is a very weak starting hand and easily dominated. I am out of position and the pot odds are not good enough to justify playing such a weak holding. Folding is the correct move.

Flash is opening from the button, and I am in the big blind with Ten-Jack offsuit. This hand has decent equity to defend against a standard button open. Calling is the most balanced strategy here, as it allows me to see a flop while maintaining a reasonable pot size, especially since I am out of position. 3-betting with this specific hand out of position is generally less optimal than playing a wider range of hands in the big blind.

I am in the Big Blind, out of position against the button aggressor. On a board of 7s-2c-5c, my Jc-Td has two overcards and a backdoor flush draw. As the pre-flop caller, the standard strategic move is to check to the original raiser. A donk bet is not advisable here, so I will check and evaluate the turn based on the opponent's action.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on a board of 7-2-5. After my pre-flop raise was called by the Big Blind, the opponent checked to me. This is a clear value-betting situation. I want to get called by weaker pairs, straight draws, or flush draws. A bet of approximately half the pot is standard here to protect my equity and extract value.

The opponent has made a continuation bet on a board of 7s-2c-5c. I am holding Jc-Td, which gives me a flush draw (Jc) and two overcards. Given the pot odds (300 to win 850), I have more than enough equity to justify a call. Folding would be too conservative given my outs, and raising is unnecessary as I am out of position and would prefer to see the turn card to potentially hit my flush or improve with an overcard.

I am out of position against the aggressor (Flash) who led the action pre-flop and on the flop. The turn card is the 5d, changing the board to 7s-2c-5c-5d. My hand (Jc-Td) has no pair and only backdoor potential, making it very weak in this spot. As the out-of-position player, the standard and correct strategic action is to check to the aggressor to evaluate their bet, or take a free card if they check behind. Donk betting is not advisable here.

On the turn, the five pairing the board is unlikely to have improved Flashlight's hand unless they were holding a five, which is less probable given their check-call on the flop. My top pair with a King kicker remains a strong hand. I will bet again to extract value from draws (like clubs or straight draws) and weaker pairs (like a lower seven or a small pocket pair). A bet of roughly half the pot should continue to build the pot while giving me information based on their reaction.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc-Td on a board of 7s-2c-5c-5d. The pot is 1750, and I am facing a 600 chip bet from the button. My hand has no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. My previous assessment regarding a flush draw was incorrect, as I only hold one club. Without any realistic chance of improving to the best hand against a range that includes any pair, calling would be mathematically incorrect. Therefore, I must fold.

Holding 9-2 offsuit (9d 2h) on the Button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With two players still to act behind me, the correct standard play is to fold and conserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Seven suited, a strong hand for heads-up play against the Big Blind after the Button folded. Raising is the optimal strategy to capture the blinds and take the initiative in the hand, given my position and hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 5h. Flashlight, in the Small Blind, has raised to 2.5x. While I am getting decent odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 400), 85o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an aggressive opponent. I have a healthy stack and see no reason to defend with such a marginal hand this early in the tournament.

7-3 offsuit is a very weak starting hand with very little potential to make a strong hand. Even with the positional advantage of the button, it is not worth investing chips to enter the pot. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Holding 7c 3d in the small blind after the button has folded is a clear fold. This hand is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially being out of position against 'Pro' in the big blind. There is no need to defend or attempt a bluff with such a low-equity hand.

I am on the button with J8offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a marginal but playable hand to open from the button, but given the dynamic where both Pro and Flashlight have been aggressive (Flashlight raised to 900 over a 250 open in hand 5, and Flash raised in hand 2), I will choose to fold this weak holding to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position or dealing with a 3-bet. Folding is a safe way to wait for a better spot given my healthy stack.

With King-Seven suited in the Small Blind and the action folded to me, I have a strong enough hand to raise and try to steal the blinds. Raising puts pressure on the Big Blind, and even if called, my hand has good playability post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind with suited connector J7s against a raise from the Small Blind. Given the stack depths and position, defending with this hand is standard practice. Folding would be too passive, and 3-betting is unnecessary risk. I will call to see a flop.

I flopped top two pair (Kings and Sevens) on a coordinated board that features a flush draw and potential straight draws. It's crucial to bet here to build the pot for value and to charge my opponent for drawing. A bet sizing of around 2/3 pot will extract value from worse Kings and draws while giving them unfavorable odds to continue.

I have middle pair (sevens) and a flush draw on a [6h 7c Kh] board. The pot odds are favorable for a call. Raising is risky given the potential that the opponent has a strong King or a better draw, and folding would be a mistake given my significant equity. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate my position.

The turn brings a Queen, making the board 6h 7c Kh Qd. While KQ now beats my Kings and Sevens, there are still many weaker hands in my opponent's range that will call a bet, such as KJ, KT, K9, as well as various flush and straight draws (like AhJh, 89). I need to continue betting to extract value from these hands and deny free equity to drawing hands. A bet slightly over half pot serves both purposes well.

On the turn with the board showing [6h 7c Kh Qd], my pair of sevens is extremely vulnerable. The opponent's bet of 800 into a 1400 pot suggests they have a King or a Queen, or potentially a straight draw they are semibluffing with. I do not have a flush draw (only three hearts total) or any significant redraws. Calling here is -EV as I am likely behind and have limited outs to improve to the best hand against a value-betting range. Therefore, folding is the disciplined choice.
I am on the Button with Jack-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand. In a 3-handed game, this hand does not have enough equity to justify opening the pot. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

With Queen-Six offsuit in the Small Blind, I am out of position against the Big Blind and have a weak hand that does not play well post-flop. Given the 100BB stack depths, there is no need to engage in a speculative blind-vs-blind battle with such a mediocre holding. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am dealt a weak hand, 9-5 offsuit, in the button position. With two opponents to act behind me and limited potential for this hand, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play. There is no incentive to defend or raise with such a marginal holding.

In a blind-vs-blind situation with Flashlight folding on the button, King-Seven suited is a very strong hand. I want to raise to put pressure on the Big Blind (Pro) and either take the blinds immediately or build a pot where I have a strength advantage. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here.

J6 suited is a weak hand and faces a 3x open from the Small Blind. Despite having position post-flop, the hand lacks high card strength and playability to profitably call a 300 chip raise. Folding is the most straightforward and EV-preserving play.

I am on the button with 83 hearts. While suited, this is a very weak hand to open with even in a three-handed game. I've had success opening from the button in previous hands, but I'll fold here to maintain a solid range and avoid playing a weak hand out of position post-flop if I get called.

With T9o in the small blind and the button folding, I have a marginal hand. While I could attempt a steal or a limp, Flashlight has shown a tendency to defend the big blind against my opens. Playing a mediocre offsuit connector out of position isn't highly profitable, so I'll fold and look for a better spot.

K9 offsuit is a marginal hand on the button. Given that both opponents have shown a willingness to 3-bet aggressively pre-flop (Flashlight to 900 in Hand 5, Flash to 850 in Hand 2), stealing with easily dominated offsuit broadways is less appealing. Folding saves chips for better spots.

The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. Q-T offsuit is a strong enough hand in a blind-versus-blind situation to open-raise and attempt to steal the pot. By raising to 300, I put pressure on the big blind and maintain the initiative, which is a standard aggressive play to accumulate chips in this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six suited (Jc 6c). The Small Blind has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more to see the flop, with the total pot being 600. This gives me 3:1 pot odds. Against a Small Blind's wide opening range, J6s has decent equity and is worth a call to see a flop, especially since I am closing the pre-flop action.

I have top pair with a Queen kicker on a dry, non-threatening board. This is a prime opportunity to continuation bet to build the pot and extract value from worse hands like lower pairs or gutshot draws. A bet of 300 into the 600-chip pot is appropriate to assert initiative while maintaining pot control.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six of clubs (Jc 6c). Pre-flop, I called a raise from Flashlight in the Small Blind. The flop comes 3s-Th-4c, which misses my hand completely, giving me only two overcards and no real draws. Flashlight has made a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. Given that the board is quite dry and I have nothing but backdoors, calling would be unprofitable against a range that likely contains better high cards or pairs. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the button position with Queen-Three offsuit. This is a very weak hand in a 3-handed game. Opening with such a poor hand from the button is suboptimal and provides little equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot to enter a pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 9s. The Button has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Pro). 89o is a reasonably strong hand for a heads-up situation from the blinds. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If called, I have a hand that can flop well.

We are facing a 3x open from the Small Blind while sitting in the Big Blind. While we will have positional advantage post-flop, 95-suited is simply too weak and disconnected to defend against a standard raise, even in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Folding is the optimal and mathematically sound decision to preserve our stack.

I am on the button with 76o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening candidate to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I have had success opening the button in previous hands (Hand 10, Hand 15, Hand 18) and taking the pot either pre-flop or with continuation bets. I will raise to 2.5bb to maintain pressure.
Facing a button raise with the worst starting hand in poker (3c2d offsuit) from the small blind, this is an automatic and trivial fold.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Seven suited, a reasonable hand to defend against a button open. Given the pot odds, calling is the most efficient play to see a flop and maintain my defensive range without overcommitting with a marginal hand.

I am out of position in the big blind with a queen-high flush draw on an Ace-high board. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here. This allows me to gauge their continuation bet frequency and control the size of the pot while realizing my equity if checked back, or potentially check-calling if the bet sizing is reasonable to chase my flush draw.

I opened pre-flop with 7d6s and was called by Flashlight in the Big Blind. The flop comes As-9s-5d, which is a very good board for my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, even though it doesn't hit my specific hand well. I have an inside straight draw (8 would give me an open-ender, but currently I just have a gutshot to the 8 and some backdoor possibilities). Flashlight checked, which is standard. A continuation bet here is highly effective because I have all the strongest Aces in my range (AK, AQ, AJ) and several sets, while the Big Blind is often capped. I will bet around 50% of the pot to exert pressure and try to take the pot down immediately.

I hold a Queen-high flush draw on an Ace-high board. The opponent has bet roughly half the pot. Given the pot odds of approximately 3:1, calling is a mathematically sound decision to realize my equity. Raising is likely too aggressive here because the opponent's betting range often includes an Ace, which dominates my hand, so I prefer to keep the pot manageable and chase my flush draw.

I am in the big blind out of position on the turn with a Queen-high flush draw. The board is ace-high, and I do not have the nut flush draw. Checking is the most strategic play here to control the pot size and allow me to realize my equity. If the opponent checks behind, I get a free card, and if they bet, I can re-evaluate based on the pot odds.

The turn card is the Jack of Spades, which connects well with the board and potentially improves my opponent's calling range from the flop (which likely included flush draws or weak Aces). Since I have 7-high and only a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8), my showdown value is non-existent. However, betting again into an Ace-high board where the opponent has already called one bet is risky, as players are generally sticky with any Ace in 3-handed play. Flashlight's check on the turn is standard. By checking back, I realize my equity for free and keep the pot manageable, giving me the opportunity to either hit my straight or potentially bluff on the river if the flush draw misses and the board texture changes favorably.

I hold pocket Kings, an incredibly strong hand. The opponent has 4-bet from the small blind, signaling either high-strength holdings like AK/QQ or a potential bluff, but given their stack size (3220 total), the stack-to-pot ratio is extremely low. Shoving all-in is the most effective way to maximize value against hands like AK or QQ that might call, and it prevents the opponent from seeing a flop where they might fold to a continuation bet. Given my stack size, this is a clear commitment.

With pocket Aces, I hold the best possible starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Facing a 5-bet all-in shove from the Big Blind, calling for my remaining stack is the clear and only optimal move. This allows me to maximize value against their entire shoving range, which likely includes hands like KK, QQ, JJ, or AK that I currently dominate.
I am on the Button with 4d 3c, which is a very weak offsuit hand. There is no reason to put chips into the pot with this trash, so the standard and optimal play is to fold.

Pro has folded on the button, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. With Jack-Eight offsuit, I have a playable hand in a heads-up blind battle, but it is not strong enough to warrant a raise given my shorter stack and the opponent's aggressive history. Completing the small blind is a standard, low-risk way to see a flop and keep the pot manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks 7s. Pro has folded on the button, and Flashlight in the Small Blind has completed the bet. Ks 7s is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. While I could raise to take control of the pot, checking allows me to see a flop for free with a hand that has good playability and ensures I maintain position throughout the rest of the hand without bloating the pot unnecessarily against the shortest stack.

I am in the Small Blind and checked the flop heads-up against the Big Blind. The board (Ace, King, Seven) is very strong for a typical Big Blind calling range, and my Jack-high hand has absolutely no showdown value or significant equity. Betting here would be a pure bluff into a board that favors the opponent's range, which is inefficient. Checking is the standard play to minimize losses and re-evaluate if the opponent checks back or to fold to any significant bet.

I have a pair of Kings on an Ace-high board. Since Flashlight checked, I will put out a small bet for value and protection. While the Ace is a scare card, my King is likely the best hand if the opponent doesn't have an Ace. Betting helps define the hand and prevents giving free cards to potential draws like clubs.

The flop of Ace-King-Seven is highly coordinated and heavily favors the Big Blind's range, which typically contains a significant number of Ace and King high hands. My hand, Jack-Eight offsuit, has zero showdown value and practically no equity against a betting range on this board. Calling a bet here is strategically unsound as I am likely drawing thin or dead, so I will fold and preserve my remaining stack for a better spot.

My holding is 9-4 suited, which is a very weak hand with poor playability, even in a 3-handed game on the button. There is no strategic value in calling or raising with such a marginal hand; preserving my stack is the priority here.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-5 offsuit. Since Flashlight has folded, it is just me and Pro (Big Blind). While it only costs 50 chips to complete the bet, 9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Against a competent opponent like Pro, I prefer to fold this hand rather than play a potentially difficult pot out of position.

I am on the button with King-Six offsuit. In a 3-handed game, this is a standard hand to open for a raise to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard sizing for this position.

Facing a 3bb open from the Button while in the Small Blind, A8o is too weak to call profitably as we will be out of position post-flop and dominated by many Aces in the Button's raising range. 3-betting as a bluff is an option, but A8o lacks the equity and playability needed for a standard 3-bet compared to suited Aces or better Broadway hands. The most standard and conservative play here is to simply fold.

Holding [Td, 2s], I have a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low card values. While the pot odds are attractive to call 200 to win 450, playing such a weak hand out of position against an aggressive opponent like Flash is negative expected value. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand.
K6o is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button, it is generally unprofitable to open with a hand this disconnected and easily dominated. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Q6s after the button folded. In a heads-up scenario, this is a playable hand. Since I have already contributed 50 to the pot, I only need to call 50 more to see the flop. Calling is the most balanced approach here, as Q6s is not strong enough to warrant a raise against the big blind's potentially wider range, but it is too valuable to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K9o. The Button folded and Flashlight (Small Blind) completed the blind. K9o is a strong hand in a blind-vs-blind situation, significantly ahead of a typical completion range. I will raise to 350 to take control of the pot and punish the limp, aiming to either win the pot immediately or play a heads-up pot with the range advantage and initiative.

The opponent, in the Big Blind, has shown a pattern of aggressive pre-flop raising throughout the session. Calling their raise with Queen-Six suited while out of position is a negative expected value play, as I will likely be dominated by their range and face difficult post-flop decisions without having the initiative. Given the current pot odds and the aggressive nature of the opponent, folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 6h 4h. This is a speculative hand, but in a steal position, it is often profitable to raise to try and pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. Given I am the short stack, stealing the blinds is important to maintain my stack viability without showdowns. I will raise to 300 to apply pressure.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 5h. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. This is a weak-medium hand in a 3-way pot. Calling out of position against a Button raiser with 8-high offsuit is generally unprofitable, and a 3-bet would be a complete bluff against an opponent who has shown aggression. Given the stack sizes and my position, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better spots.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, T9 offsuit is too weak to defend from the Big Blind. While suited connectors are defendable, offsuit connectors like T9o suffer from poor realization out of position and should generally be folded to a 3bb raise.

I have been dealt pocket Kings, which is an extremely strong premium hand. My goal is to build a large pot while I have a significant equity advantage. Since I am on the button, I will put in a standard open-raise of 3x the big blind to 300. This invites action from the blinds while maintaining control of the betting lead.

I am in the Small Blind with 87o, which is a weak offsuit connector. The Button has opened to 3 big blinds. Playing this hand out of position is unprofitable, and it doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify calling or 3-betting as a bluff, especially with the Big Blind still left to act. The standard and most optimal decision is to fold.

I am in the big blind with suited cards Js 7s, getting decent pot odds of roughly 2.25:1 (200 to call for a 450 pot). This makes calling a profitable play against an open from the button. Folding would be too passive given the stack depth, and raising is not optimal with this holding.

The flop is very dry, paired with fives, and I have only Jack-high. Being in the big blind out of position against the button, checking is the standard play here to evaluate the opponent's strategy. I have no reason to lead out with such a weak hand.

I have pocket Kings on a very dry 5-2-5 rainbow board. While there is a small risk the opponent has a 5, I am almost certainly ahead. I want to extract value from lower pocket pairs or even Ace-high hands that might float. A continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot is effective here to build the pot and get value from the opponent's calling range.

The opponent (Flash) has made a small continuation bet of 250 into a 650 pot on a very dry board (5h, 2s, 5d). With Jack-high (Js, 7s), I have some overcard equity and the pot odds are favorable (roughly 3.6:1). Folding here is too weak given the small sizing, and raising with air would be a reckless bluff. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card cheaply.

I am in the big blind on the turn with Jack-high. The board of 5h-2s-5d-8s is uncoordinated, but I am out of position and have no pair. Betting here would be a pure bluff against a range that already called a flop bet and likely has me beaten. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain control of the pot size and see if my opponent checks back, allowing me to potentially realize my equity or get to a cheap showdown.

I hold pocket Kings on a board of 5-2-5-8. My opponent, Flashlight, called pre-flop and called a flop continuation bet. The 8 on the turn is relatively blank, although it could have improved a small portion of their range (like A8 or 88, though 88 is unlikely). I still have an extremely strong hand that is ahead of most of Flashlight's calling range, such as mid-pocket pairs (66-TT) or Ace-high floats. I will bet for value to build the pot and charge those hands, aiming for about 60% of the pot.

Flash has bet 700 into a pot of 1800 on the turn. With my hand of Jack of spades and Seven of spades, and a board containing the Two of spades and Eight of spades, I have a flush draw (four spades total). This gives me nine outs to hit a flush on the river, providing me with significant equity. While I do not have a made hand, the pot odds of calling 700 to win 1800 make this a mathematically sound continuation. Given our stack depth, I have sufficient implied odds to chase the draw, and calling allows me to realize this equity at the best price, avoiding a high-variance bluff-raise against an aggressive opponent.

The board is 5h-2s-5d-8s-5c. The pot is 2550. My hand is Js-7s. I missed my spade flush draw and I am holding only Jack-high on a board where any pair (or any Ace/King high) likely has me beat. Flash has been aggressive throughout the hand, betting both the flop and turn, so he is very likely to have a made hand that will call a bet. Bluffing here would be highly unprofitable as I have no realistic chance to make a better hand fold. Checking is the only sensible play to reach showdown as cheaply as possible, or to minimize further losses.

I have a very strong hand, Kings full of Fives (Ks Kh 5h 5d 5c). The board runout is 5-2-5-8-5. The only hands that beat me are the case 5 (quads) or pocket Aces (Aces full of Fives). Given Flashlight's line of check-calling on the flop and turn, it is highly likely they hold a mid-pocket pair or perhaps an Ace-high float. With the third 5 landing on the river, any pocket pair Flashlight holds now makes a full house (Fives full of their pair), making them much more likely to call a value bet. I will bet approximately half the pot to extract value from these inferior full houses.

The board shows three fives, and I hold only Jack-high with no pair. Flash's river bet of 1300 indicates strength, and there is no scenario where Jack-high beats any value hand that would bet this board. My hand has zero showdown value, and bluffing is not viable. Folding is the only logical play.

Holding 94 suited on the button is an extremely weak starting hand. While a button steal can be profitable with marginal hands, 94s is simply too disconnected and low to warrant an open raise. The best and most standard action here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind. Completing the small blind with T3 offsuit is standard given the pot odds and position, allowing me to see a flop cheaply despite the hand's weakness.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. The Button folded and the Small Blind completed the bet. Since I have a marginal hand and will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand, checking to see a free flop is the most sensible play. This avoids bloating the pot with a mediocre holding while allowing me to utilize my position post-flop.

The flop is Q-Q-5, and I have 10-3 offsuit, which completely missed the board. As the small blind who completed pre-flop, checking is the most prudent action. I have no showdown value and no reasonable semi-bluff equity here. I will check to see the opponent's action and potentially fold if they make a significant bet.

I have Jack-high on a paired board (Qc Qd 5s). Flashlight completed from the small blind and checked the flop. There is no reason to bet here; I have some showdown value against complete air and very little equity if I get called or raised. Checking back keeps the pot small and allows me to see a free turn card in position.

The board is Q-Q-5-2 and my 10-3 offsuit has no value or drawing potential. Given that the flop was checked around, it is highly likely that neither of us has a strong hand, but representing a Queen is not viable with these cards. Checking is the standard play to keep the pot small and minimize potential losses, as any bet I make will likely only be called by a hand that already beats me.

I have Jack-high on a board that is unlikely to have hit either of our ranges significantly, though the pair of Queens makes it tricky. Flashlight has checked twice now (flop and turn) after completing from the Small Blind. Since I have some showdown value against complete air and the pot is small, checking back is the most prudent play to see a free river and control the size of the pot.

The board is Q-Q-5-2-4, and I hold T-3. I have T-high with no showdown value, as even a pair of 2s, 4s, or 5s would beat me. Since the hand was checked down to the river, it is highly likely that neither player has a strong hand, but checking is the safest and most rational play. Betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from even weak pairs.

The board is Qc Qd 5s 2d 4d. Flashlight completed from the small blind and has checked every street. I have Jack-high, which has some marginal showdown value against complete air but will not be called by anything worse. Since the pot is only 200 chips, there is no incentive to bluff, as I am unlikely to fold out a small pair or an Ace-high. Checking back to see the showdown is the most consistent and low-risk play.

As the button in a three-handed game, I have a significant positional advantage. Holding an Ace-deuce, I have a reasonable enough hand to attempt to steal the blinds and put pressure on my opponents. Since I am currently the short stack, I need to be proactive in accumulating chips, and a raise from the button is a standard, strategic move to widen my range and force folds from the blinds.

I have pocket fours in the Small Blind. Flashlight raised to 300 from the Button. Pocket pairs are excellent set-mining hands, and the price to call (250 more into a 450 pot) is favorable. While a 3-bet is possible to take the initiative, calling allows me to see a flop cheaply and evaluate. If the Big Blind (Pro) decides to squeeze, I will have to reconsider, but for now, calling is the most standard play with a small pocket pair in this position.

Holding 7-3 offsuit, one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand, especially out of position. The only correct move is to fold.

I have pocket fours on a very unfavorable flop of Ad-Ks-Kc. This board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which likely includes many Aces and Kings. My hand has very little potential for improvement and is currently just a weak pair that is likely behind. Checking is the most standard and prudent play here, allowing me to see how the Button responds to this texture without committing more chips to a pot where I am a significant underdog.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker on a paired board (A-K-K). The board is extremely dangerous because any King beats me, and any Ace with a kicker higher than a deuce dominates me. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or induce a check-raise that forces me to fold. Checking behind is the safest play to keep the pot small, control the variance, and reach a cheap showdown.

The board (Ad-Ks-Kc-Js) is extremely coordinated and favors almost any Broadway cards in the opponent's Button opening range. My pocket fours are now effectively a bluff-catcher with very little value. After both players checked the flop, the Jack of Spades on the turn completes many potential straight draws (QT) or gives the opponent a pair of Jacks. Since I have no real equity to improve and my hand is likely beaten by even a marginal holding from the opponent, checking is the only logical play. I am looking to see a free river or fold to any significant aggression.

The board is Ad-Ks-Kc-Js. I hold Ac-2s. While I have top pair, my kicker is the lowest possible, and the presence of Kings and a Jack on the board creates many combinations that have me beat (any King, any two pair, or broadway straight draws). Betting into my opponent would likely only be called by better hands or induce a raise, forcing me off my equity. Checking behind is the optimal play to control the pot size and reach a cheap showdown.

On a board of Ad-Ks-Kc-Js-5h, my pocket fours have very little showdown value. While Flashlight's checks on the flop and turn suggest weakness, I am likely still behind any Broadway cards or higher pocket pairs that they may be holding. Betting is unlikely to force a fold from a better hand like a Jack or a medium pair, and I am certainly not getting called by worse hands. Checking is the most sensible option to either reach a cheap showdown or fold if Flashlight decides to bet.

I have reached the river with top pair (A-2) on a board of Ad-Ks-Kc-Js-5h. My kicker is the worst possible, and the board contains many cards (Kings, Jacks, and any Ace with a kicker higher than a deuce) that have me beat. Furthermore, a ten-queen combination would have made a straight. Betting for value is impossible as I am only called by better hands. Checking behind is the only way to minimize risk and take the showdown value I currently have, hoping that my opponent is also checking down a medium-strength or bluff-catching hand.

I am on the button with pocket sixes (6d, 6s) in a 3-handed game. This is a very strong hand to open with. I've been successful with opening raises of 300 in previous hands to take control of the pot. With Flashlight being the short stack in the Big Blind, a standard raise puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to either win the pot immediately or play a pot in position with a solid pocket pair.
I am in the Small Blind with an unsuited connector (5d 6h), facing a 3x raise from the Button. This is a very weak hand to play out of position, so folding is the optimal and standard play here.

I am in the big blind holding 4c-2d, which is a very weak hand with poor playability. Flash has raised to 300, and calling would require investing 200 more into a pot where I am out of position against a strong range. Given the weak nature of my hole cards, the most disciplined and +EV move is to fold and wait for a better spot.
Holding Ten-Four offsuit on the Button, this hand is extremely weak and far outside any viable opening range. The best and standard action here is to fold.

Holding Q6 offsuit in the small blind, I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call against the big blind. While the hand is marginal, completing the small blind is a standard play here to see a cheap flop, especially with the button having folded.

I am in the big blind with 3h 2h. The small blind (Flashlight) has completed the blind by limping. While my hand is suited, it is extremely weak. Since I can see the flop for free, there is no reason to raise and risk chips with one of the worst starting hands in poker. Checking is the most standard and safe play here.

I have top pair with a very weak kicker (six). The board is relatively wet, containing a flush draw and potential straight draws. Betting into the big blind here would likely only get called by better Queens or better hands, while folding out the hands I am currently beating. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate on the turn, as I am out of position.

I have absolutely nothing on this flop (Qs 5d Jd) with my 3h 2h. The small blind (Flashlight) has checked. There is no reason to put any chips in the pot with zero equity and no real draw. I will check and hope to see a free turn card.

I hold a pair of Queens with a weak six kicker. The board is now extremely wet with three diamonds and an Ace, making it highly probable that my opponent has at least an Ace or a flush draw. Betting would be overplaying my hand, as I would likely only be called by better hands. Checking allows me to keep the pot small and see a river card safely.

I have 3-high on a board of Qs-5d-Jd-Ad. I have no pair and no draws. Both Flashlight and I have checked through the flop and now Flashlight has checked the turn. There is no reason to bluff into a tiny pot with zero equity. I will check and take the free card.

The board has become extremely dangerous with a four-flush present on the river, along with straight possibilities (like King-Ten). My two pair (Queens and Sixes) is a very marginal holding here and functions only as a bluff-catcher. Given that both my opponent and I have checked through every street, it is highly likely that neither of us has a strong made hand, but betting now would only induce calls from better hands (like any Ace or any diamond flush) and fold out hands that I am currently beating. Therefore, checking is the most prudent action to reach showdown and minimize potential losses.

The board has four diamonds on it (5d, Jd, Ad, 6d). I have absolutely nothing (3-high, no diamonds). Flashlight has checked every street after limping pre-flop. While I could try to bluff representing a diamond, the pot is only 200 chips. Risking a bluff into a board where the opponent might call with any small diamond or even a weak pair is not worth the potential loss of stack in such a small pot. I will check and concede the hand.

With Ace-Six offsuit on the button, I have a reasonable hand to open with in a three-handed game. My stack is relatively short (50BB), so I should be looking to pick up the blinds when possible. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size to potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand in position.

I am in the Small Blind holding KdJh. Flashlight has raised from the Button to 300. This is a standard late-position open. KdJh is a strong hand that is well ahead of a typical button opening range. I could call to keep the pot smaller, but since Flashlight has a relatively short stack (4785 total), I want to put pressure on them and build a pot while I have the range advantage. A 3-bet to 950 is a standard size that forces out the Big Blind and puts Flashlight in a tough position, likely leading to a fold or a call with a dominated hand. If Flashlight pushes back, I can re-evaluate based on their stack size and the action from Pro.
Facing an open raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, my Ten-Six offsuit in the Big Blind is completely unplayable. It's an easy fold.

With Ace-Six offsuit, I am in a tough spot against a 3-bet from the Small Blind. While I have position, A6o is a dominated hand against most 3-betting ranges, and calling out of position or even in position against an aggressive 3-bettor often leads to difficult post-flop situations. Given my stack size and the strength of a typical 3-bet range, the most prudent strategic decision is to fold and preserve my stack for a better, more advantageous spot.

On the button with QJo, I have a strong opening hand for a 3-handed game. I will raise to 3x the big blind (300) to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. Flashlight is the shorter stack and Pro is a capable opponent, but QJo is well within the standard raising range here to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am dealt 5s 2d in the small blind, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the button, my hand has virtually no playability or equity, and I would be out of position post-flop. Folding is the only viable option.

I am in the big blind with a small pocket pair (55). The button has raised to 300. Calling here offers favorable pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 450). With a stack of 4685, set-mining is a viable and standard strategy against a button open, as the pair holds reasonable equity heads-up and can be very profitable if I hit a set.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket fives on a King-high board. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor, and my hand has very little equity against their range, essentially functioning as a bluff catcher that is behind almost all hands they would bet for value. There is no reason to lead out here. Checking is the standard and correct play to control the pot size and re-evaluate their action.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop has come out King-high and very dry (Ks 4h 6d). This is an excellent board for a continuation bet because my perceived range contains many more Kings than the Big Blind's calling range. Flashlight checked to me, which is expected. I will place a standard c-bet of approximately 38% of the pot (250) to put pressure on his middle pairs or missed hands. If he folds, I win the pot immediately; if he calls, I still have two overcards to the middle of the board and some backdoor straight potential.

The board texture is K-4-6, which is reasonably dry but hits a standard button-opening range well. I hold 5-5, giving me a gutshot straight draw to a 3 or a 7 (8 outs). With a pot of 650 and a bet of 250, I am getting pot odds of roughly 3.6 to 1, which justifies a call. I am also currently ahead of any pure bluff attempts from the opponent, though I am likely behind any King or better high-card hand. Calling allows me to realize my equity and re-evaluate on the turn.

The board texture is K-4-6-6, which is very dry and highly favorable to the pre-flop aggressor's range. My pair of fives is extremely weak on this board; I am behind any King, any Six, and any pocket pair higher than fives. I have effectively no showdown value against an opponent's standard opening range. Checking is the only logical play to control the pot size and re-evaluate. If the opponent bets, I will likely need to fold.

The turn card is the Six of Clubs, which pairs the bottom card of the flop. Flashlight checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. My Queen-Jack high currently has limited showdown value, but I have two overcards to the middle of the board. If Flashlight was calling the flop with a middle pair (like 77-TT) or a Six, he has now improved or remains ahead. If he had a King, he is unlikely to fold to a second barrel. Given the board texture and Flashlight's check-call on the flop, checking back here is the most prudent play to control the pot size and potentially realize my equity if a Queen or Jack hits the river, or to evaluate based on his action on the final street.

The river Ace is a very bad card for my pocket fives. My hand is effectively a bluff catcher with almost no showdown value against the opponent's betting range. Betting would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to force a fold from any hand that has equity. Checking is the standard play to minimize losses, re-evaluate, or check-fold if the opponent makes a bet.

The board has run out King-Four-Six-Six-Ace. I was the pre-flop aggressor and continuation bet the flop. Flashlight's check-call on the flop followed by checks on the turn and river suggests a marginal made hand like a weak King, a pair of fours, or a mid-pocket pair. My Queen-Jack high has no showdown value and will lose to any pair. The Ace on the river is a significant scare card that is much more likely to be in my range than Flashlight's, as I raised from the button. By betting roughly 56% of the pot, I can represent an Ace or a strong King that was checking the turn for pot control, putting maximum pressure on Flashlight's capped range.

The river Ace pairs the board and strengthens the opponent's range significantly, as they likely have an Ace or a King in their pre-flop raising range. My pocket fives are effectively a weak bluff-catcher, losing to any Ace, any King, any Six, or any higher pocket pair. With the opponent betting 650 into a 1800 pot, I am not getting the pot odds to justify a call, and I have almost zero equity against their value-betting range. Therefore, folding is the correct and disciplined decision.

I am dealt Jack-Two offsuit on the button, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Playing this hand would be highly unprofitable, so the standard and correct play is to fold pre-flop and conserve my stack.

Pro has folded on the button, so it is just me in the small blind against the big blind. With 6s-5h, I have a decent enough hand to complete the small blind and see a flop. Calling is standard in this blind-vs-blind situation to see the action cheaply given the pot odds.

I am in the Big Blind with King-five offsuit. The button has folded and the Small Blind has limped in. King-five is a marginal hand that doesn't benefit significantly from raising in this spot, especially against a short stack who might call or shove. Checking allows me to see the flop for free and realize my equity without increasing the pot size unnecessarily with a mediocre holding.

I am in the small blind with 6-high on an Ace-high board (A-Q-7). My hand has zero equity here, no pair, and no draw. Checking is the only sensible move. Betting into the Big Blind on this texture is likely to be called by any Ace or Queen, and represents a bluff that is rarely successful given the board hits the Big Blind's defending range well. I will check and plan to fold to any significant aggression.

I have King-high on an Ace-high board in a limped pot. Flashlight checked to me. There is no reason to bet here as I have some showdown value against total bluffs and very little fold equity against any hand that has a piece of this board (any pair, any Ace, any Queen). By checking, I realize my equity for free and keep the pot small with a marginal holding.

I am in the small blind with 6-high on a board of A-A-Q-7. My hand has effectively zero equity and no showdown value. Bluffing into the Big Blind on this board texture is highly unlikely to be effective, as the paired Ace strongly hits their defending range. Checking is the optimal play to keep the pot small and control the size of the bet I might face later.

I am in the Big Blind with King-high in a limped pot. The board now has a pair of Aces, which significantly reduces the likelihood that either of us has a strong hand unless one of us is holding an Ace. Flashlight (Small Blind) has checked the flop and now the turn. I have some showdown value against complete air, and there is no benefit to betting here as I am unlikely to fold out better hands like a Queen or a Seven, and I have nothing to gain by bloating the pot with King-high. Checking back allows me to see the river for free.

I hold 6-high on a board of A-A-Q-8-7. My hand has absolutely no showdown value and will lose to any pair or better, which constitutes the entirety of the opponent's defending range. Checking is the only logical action; betting would be an unsuccessful bluff, and I must accept that this hand will lose at showdown.
