My hand still has some showdown value and outs to improve (a King or Jack), so taking another free card to see the river is the most prudent and pot-controlling play.

The board has three clubs, making a potential flush, and a straight is also possible with the 5-8-6-T combination. My pair of deuces is extremely weak and provides essentially no showdown value in a three-way pot. Betting would only get called by better hands, as I am essentially bluffing into two opponents with no equity. Checking allows me to see if the hand goes to a showdown cheaply or to fold if one of the opponents decides to bet.

The board has completed with three clubs and a Ten. Given that the hand has been checked all the way through the flop and turn, it is highly unlikely that anyone holds a significant hand. While my King-high has very little showdown value in a three-way pot, betting here to represent a flush (especially since I hold the Queen of clubs as a blocker) is a possibility. However, in a tournament setting with this stack depth and against these opponents, checking to see if I can get a free showdown is the most prudent move. If I check and Pro (the original raiser) also checks, I might occasionally win against other high-card hands. If someone bets, I can safely fold knowing King-high is rarely good when facing late-street action.

With King-high and no pair or flush on a board with three clubs, my hand has very little showdown value against two opponents. However, bluffing into two players who have shown passivity throughout the hand is risky, as one of them could easily be trapping with a pair, a weak club, or just decide to hero-call with Ace-high. Taking the free showdown is the safest and most pot-controlling play.

I am in the button position with a very weak hand, 8-2 suited. In a three-handed game, while positional advantage exists, this hand does not have enough equity to justify calling a raise or entering the pot. Preserving my stack of 7120 is more important than playing a marginal hand, so folding is the disciplined choice.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The button (Flashlight) has folded. While I could attempt to steal the Big Blind with a raise, Jack-Six offsuit is a very weak hand that lacks post-flop playability and equity. Given that I am the chip leader and there are only 19 hands remaining in the tournament, I prefer to play a disciplined game and avoid over-extending with marginal hands out of position against a solid opponent. Therefore, folding is the most prudent action.

I am on the button with King-high suited (Kd 6d), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I have the chip lead and the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. Opening with a standard raise to 300 is the correct play here to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If either opponent calls, I will be playing a suited King in position, which is a favorable situation.

I am in the Small Blind with 87o, a weak offsuit connector. The Button (Flash), who is the chip leader, has opened for 3x the big blind. Calling here is unprofitable because I would be playing out of position with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop, and I still have the Big Blind behind me who could squeeze. 3-betting as a bluff is also suboptimal given the hand strength. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice.

King-three suited is a reasonable hand to defend from the big blind against a button open. Given the pot odds and the fact that I am closing the action pre-flop, calling to see a flop is the most efficient way to play this hand while maintaining a balanced defense range.

I am in the big blind with King-high and a backdoor flush draw on an Ace-high board. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play to evaluate their continuation bet. I have minimal showdown value and limited equity, so I should proceed cautiously.

I opened pre-flop and Flashlight called from the big blind. The flop comes A-Q-2 rainbow, which is an excellent board for a continuation bet. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have all the strongest hands (AA, QQ, AQ, AK) in my range. Flashlight checked to me, which is standard. I will bet about half the pot to put pressure on his marginal holdings and likely take the pot down immediately. If called, I have King-high which has some limited showdown value if the board runs out favorably, but the primary goal is to win now.

The flop is Ace-high and very dry, and my King-high holding has essentially no showdown value. With only a backdoor flush draw, my equity is insufficient to continue against a continuation bet. Folding is the most disciplined and effective way to preserve my stack given the board texture and my lack of significant draws.
Holding Ten-Four suited on the Button is far too weak to consider opening. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Three offsuit. The button folded, so it is a heads-up situation against the big blind. The cost to complete is 50 chips to a 150 chip pot. Given the favorable pot odds and the fact that I am already committed for 50 chips, I will call to see a cheap flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d 4d. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has completed the bet. Since I have a speculative suited hand and can see the flop for free, checking is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead while still having the potential to hit a strong hand on the flop. Raising here is unnecessary given the stack depths and the stage of the tournament.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Three offsuit on a King-Jack-Six board. I have no pair and no draw. Checking is the only logical play as I am out of position and have no strength to bet into the big blind. If the opponent bets, I will be prepared to fold.

I am in the Big Blind in a limped pot against the Small Blind (Flashlight). The flop is Kd-Js-6s, and I have 7-high with a backdoor diamond flush draw. Flashlight has checked to me. Since the pot is small and I have very little showdown value but some marginal improvement potential, checking back is the most sensible play. Betting here doesn't achieve much as any King or Jack is unlikely to fold, and I can take a free card to see if I pick up a flush or straight draw on the turn.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Three offsuit on a board that now has three spades (Kd, Js, 6s, 4s). I have no pair, no draw, and no showdown value. Betting would be illogical as it would likely only be called by better hands. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach the river without further investment.

In a limped pot, I have improved to bottom pair on the turn. The board is quite coordinated with three spades and high cards (King and Jack). Flashlight has checked both the flop and the turn, indicating weakness or a marginal made hand. Since the pot is small and my hand has some showdown value but is very vulnerable to a check-raise or a lead on the river, checking back to take a free card and reach showdown cheaply is the most logical play. There is no need to turn my pair into a bluff or inflate the pot at this stage.

I am in the small blind with Queen-Three offsuit. The board is King-Jack-Six-Four-Five. I have only Queen-high and no pair, draw, or any form of showdown value. The betting has been checked around on every street up to the river. Since I have no hand, I cannot bet for value, and I have no reason to bluff as I have already checked and the opponent also checked. Checking is the only logical action to reach the showdown.

In this limped pot, the board has run out Kd-Js-6s-4s-5d. I have bottom pair (fours). Flashlight has checked on every street, including the river, which strongly indicates a weak hand or a missed draw. While a pair of fours is very weak, it has significant showdown value in a limped pot against a range that likely consists of high-card hands or very weak pairs. Betting would not likely induce a fold from a better hand (like a six or a five) and would not be called by a worse hand. Therefore, checking back to realize my equity and reach showdown for free is the most optimal play.
Holding 7-4 offsuit is a very weak hand with minimal equity. Investing 100 chips to call is not a profitable move in this position. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more favorable hand.

I am the current chip leader with a significant stack. In the Small Blind, after the Button folds, I am heads-up against Pro in the Big Blind. 7c 8d is a marginal but playable hand. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and attempt to take the pot pre-flop. If Pro re-raises, I can safely fold given the strength of my hand and my desire to preserve my lead with only 15 hands remaining.

J6o is a very weak hand, even in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Although we have position post-flop, the hand lacks playability and equity to justify calling a 3x raise from the small blind. Folding is the best choice here.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand even in 3-handed play. While opening from the button is generally aggressive, J3o is too weak to justify a raise given that both opponents are capable of defending or 3-betting. Folding allows me to preserve my chip lead and wait for a better spot.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind. I hold ATo, which is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to extract value from worse hands, put pressure on the Big Blind, and potentially take down the pot right now.

Defending the big blind against a small blind raise with a suited King is a standard play given the pot odds and the potential for a flush. While K2s is not a premium hand, it is sufficient to defend in this position.

I raised pre-flop with Ace-Ten offsuit and was called by the Big Blind. The flop comes extremely dry and low (7-2-4 rainbow), which is unlikely to have connected heavily with my opponent's calling range unless they hold a small pocket pair or hit a stray pair. My Ace-high might still be the best hand, but a continuation bet will apply pressure to fold out their random overcards (like KQ, QJ, etc.) and protect my equity. I'll bet half the pot.

I have bottom pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry, low-card board. The opponent's bet of 300 into a 900 pot is a standard continuation bet. With bottom pair, I have sufficient equity to call and see the turn, as I am likely ahead of air or unpaired overcards. Folding would be too weak, and raising doesn't accomplish much against a potential range that includes overcards or better pairs.

The turn card pairs my Ten, giving me top pair with an Ace kicker. My opponent's flop calling range consists of middle pairs like 88 or 99, a pair of sevens, and various floats or straight draws. Betting here allows me to extract value from these worse made hands and charge any newly acquired flush draws or gutshots. I will bet around two-thirds of the pot to continue building it and clearly define the strength of my hand.

My pair of deuces is extremely vulnerable on this board. The opponent has shown consistent aggression by betting both the flop and the turn. With a bet of 800 into a 1200 pot, the opponent is representing at least a pair of tens, a seven, or perhaps an overpair/draw that has realized equity. My hand has very little room to improve, and I am almost certainly behind in this spot. Calling would only commit more chips to a hand with minimal showdown value.

K6o is a very marginal hand, even from the Button in a 3-handed game. It's generally better to fold these weak offsuit Kings rather than attempting a steal and facing difficult post-flop decisions or pre-flop 3-bets.

I am in the small blind with Ace-high (A4o). The button (Pro) has folded. Raising from the small blind is a standard aggressive move to steal the blinds or play a heads-up pot against the Big Blind (Flash). An open raise to 300 is an appropriate sizing to apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket deuces (22). The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. This is a heads-up situation post-flop where I will have position. Pocket deuces is a small pair that is likely ahead of a wide Small Blind opening range, but it is very vulnerable to overcards. Calling is the most prudent play here; it allows me to see a flop efficiently, potentially set-mine, or take the pot down post-flop if the board is favorable or if Flashlight shows weakness. I have a significant chip lead and do not need to risk a large 3-bet/fold or 4-bet scenario against a stack of 58 BBs.

I hold bottom pair (a pair of 4s) on a fairly coordinated board containing a Ten and a Jack. My hand is relatively weak and serves better as a check-call or check-fold candidate to control the pot size rather than betting, as a bet is likely to be called only by hands that have me beaten or have significant equity against me. Checking allows me to evaluate the turn and potentially reach a cheap showdown.

On a board of Ts-4c-Jh, my pocket deuces are an underpair and hold very little relative strength. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor from the small blind, has checked. While I could bet to try and take the pot down now, this board hits a significant portion of a standard SB opening range (Broadway cards, pairs). By checking back, I control the size of the pot and get to see a turn card for free, preserving my stack while looking for a miracle deuce or seeing how the action develops on later streets.

I hold a pair of 4s with an Ace kicker. The board is coordinated with straight and flush draws possible (Ten, Jack, and a 3 on the turn). The opponent checked behind on the flop, which suggests they might have a weak hand, an overcard, or a draw that they wanted to take to the turn for free. Betting my bottom pair is unlikely to get value from worse hands while simultaneously putting myself in a difficult spot if I am raised. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and see the river card for free, as it preserves my stack while allowing me to reassess based on the opponent's action on the river.

On the turn, the board is Ts-4c-Jh-3c. I hold pocket deuces (22), which remains an underpair. Flashlight has checked for the second time after raising pre-flop. This check-check sequence from the aggressor often indicates a marginal hand or a complete miss with overcards. While my deuces have very little showdown value, betting into a board with two Broadway cards is risky, as Flashlight could be trapping or holding a marginal pair (like a 4 or mid-pocket pair) that won't fold to a single small bet. Since I have position and the pot is small, checking back is the safest play. It allows me to see the river for free and potentially win the pot at showdown if Flashlight has unimproved high cards like AK or KQ.

I hold a pair of 4s on a board of T-J-4-3-7. Both the opponent and I have checked through the flop and the turn, which suggests that neither of us has connected strongly with this board. My pair of 4s is very weak, but it could potentially be the best hand if my opponent is floating with complete air or a total miss. Betting here is unlikely to yield value as any hand I beat will likely fold, and any hand that calls will likely have me beaten. Therefore, checking is the optimal strategy to reach a cheap showdown and hope to win against the opponent's range of missed draws or absolute air.

On the river, the board is Ts-4c-Jh-3c-7h. I hold pocket deuces, which is an underpair to most of the board. Flashlight raised pre-flop but has checked on the flop, turn, and river. This indicates a very weak range, likely consisting of missed overcards (like AK, AQ, KQ) or perhaps a very marginal made hand that they are afraid to value bet. While my pocket deuces are weak, they do have some showdown value against a range that includes several missed high-card combinations. Since the pot is small and I have position, checking back is the most logical choice. It allows me to reach showdown for free. If Flashlight has a hand like Ace-high or King-high, I win; if they have a small pair like 55 or 66, I lose the minimum. Betting would likely only be called by hands that beat me and would fold out hands I already beat.

I am on the button with a strong suited broadway hand (AhTh). In a three-handed game, this hand is very powerful in position. To capitalize on my advantage and put pressure on the blinds, I will raise. My standard opening raise size has been 300, which is an appropriate amount to potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 3c, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button. This hand has very poor playability and strength, making it an easy fold regardless of the pot odds or the tournament stage.

Pocket fours in the Big Blind facing a Button raise is a standard defend. We are getting strong pot odds (200 to call for a pot of 450) and have good implied odds against Flashlight's remaining stack if we can flop a set. I'll flat call and evaluate on the flop.

On a flop of 9c Td 2s, I have under-represented pair (pocket fours). The standard play as the pre-flop caller out of position is to check to the initial raiser to gauge their strength and control the size of the pot.

I have top pair with top pair, ten kicker on a relatively dry board. Given I raised pre-flop and am in position, a continuation bet is standard to build value and test the strength of my opponent's hand. A bet of 300 (roughly half the pot) is appropriate here to extract value from smaller pairs or potential gutshot draws.

We defended our Big Blind with pocket fours and missed our set on a flop with two overcards (9 and T). Facing a continuation bet from the pre-flop raiser while out of position, our hand is too weak to continue. We are often already behind, and almost any turn card will be difficult to navigate. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve chips.

I am on the button with Qc 3c. This is a marginal suited hand, but in a 3-handed game, it falls within a reasonable opening range (around 45-50% of hands). With only 11 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy chip lead, I want to maintain pressure on the blinds without taking unnecessary risks. A standard 3x raise allows me to take down the blinds immediately or play the hand in position if called. My recent history shows that both Pro and Flashlight are willing to fold to button opens, though they occasionally defend or 3-bet. Given the stack sizes, I will open for 300.

I have A3o in the Small Blind facing a Button open from Flash. A3o is a weak offsuit Ace that plays poorly out of position, and the Big Blind is still left to act. The risk of being dominated by better Aces or facing a squeeze from the BB makes calling or 3-betting unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the big blind with T8o against a button raise from Flash. T8o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position and is generally not worth defending against an aggressive opponent's opening raise. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for a better spot.

K7 suited is a solid hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. By raising, I put pressure on the blinds to fold, giving me a chance to win the pot uncontested. If called, I have position and a hand that can make strong flushes and top pairs.

I am in the small blind with King-Six suited, a playable hand against a button open. With a stack of 6620 and a button raise to 250, calling is the most balanced approach to see a flop without overcommitting in an out-of-position scenario. This keeps the pot manageable and allows me to play post-flop.

In Hand #140, I'm holding Ace-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button (Pro) raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Flashlight) called. I'm getting excellent pot odds (4:1) to see a flop. While Ace-Ten is strong enough to squeeze (raise), calling is a lower-variance play that allows me to see a flop and use my chip lead to outplay my opponents post-flop, especially since the SB's range is likely capped by the flat-call. I will call the additional 150 to close the pre-flop action.

I am in the small blind in a three-way pot with a King-high flush draw. Given I am out of position against two other players, checking is the most standard and balanced play to realize my equity and keep the pot size manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. The flop of 2-J-3 with two clubs does not connect strongly with my hand, though I have one overcard (the Ace) to the Jack. The Small Blind has checked to me. Given that the Button was the original pre-flop aggressor, the standard play here is to check and see how the Button acts. Leading out into two players with only Ace-high is risky. Checking allows me to control the size of the pot and potentially see a turn card for free or evaluate the situation if the Button bets.

With King-high and no direct draws on a wet board (two clubs) against two opponents, a continuation bet is risky. Checking behind allows me to see a free turn card, potentially hitting a King or saving chips to evaluate on the next street.

With bottom pair and a King-high flush draw, I have decent equity. The field checked the flop, indicating a lack of strong made hands. Checking allows me to realize my equity for free, control the pot while out of position, and potentially hit my flush on the river, or evaluate if a river bet is profitable if I improve.

On the turn, the board is 2s-Jc-3c-6s. The flop was checked around, which suggests that neither the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) nor the Small Blind (Flashlight) has a strong hand like a Jack. With my Ace-high (As Tc), I have some showdown value and potentially the best hand, but betting into two opponents with only Ace-high is thin. By checking, I can see how Pro reacts to the weakness shown by Flashlight and me. If it goes check-check again, I get a free river card and a better chance to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate based on the river card.

With King-high and no draws on a board containing two flush draws and potential straight draws, a bluff into two opponents is risky. Since both opponents have checked twice, I have some showdown value against completely missed hands or missed draws. Taking a free card on the river is the safest and most optimal play here.

I have a pair of sixes on a board of A-J-6-3-2. The river Ace significantly strengthens the ranges of my opponents, both of whom have checked throughout the hand. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands, and I will likely only be called by better hands like an Ace or a Jack. Checking to realize my showdown value is the most prudent and balanced strategy in this spot.

The river Ace improves my hand to top pair with a Ten kicker. The action was checked through the flop and turn, which suggests that neither the Button (Pro) nor the Small Blind (Flashlight) holds a Jack or better. Since Flashlight has checked to me on the river, I will place a value bet of 400. This sizing is intended to get called by a Jack, a smaller pocket pair, or perhaps a Six, while also capitalizing on the fact that the Ace is a scare card that might represent a range-advantage lead.

I have King-high with no made hand on a board containing an Ace. After it checked through the flop and turn, Flash's river bet indicates they likely connected with the Ace, hold a made pair, or are bluffing a missed draw. Since I have no showdown value and a bluff-raise would be unnecessarily risky with zero meaningful blockers, the only profitable decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

I am facing a bet of 400 into a pot of 1150 on the river with a pair of sixes. The board is A-J-6-3-2. While the Ace on the river improves some of the opponent's range, the betting line (check-check-check through the turn) suggests that this could easily be a bluff or a weak value bet from the big blind. With pot odds of approximately 1:3.8, I only need to be right about 26% of the time to make this call profitable. My pair of sixes is a decent bluff-catcher here against a wide big-blind range, so I will call.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, particularly when in position on the button. Raising allows me to take the initiative, build the pot, and potentially fold out weaker hands. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to accomplish this, allowing me to play a controlled pot against the small and big blinds.

I am in the small blind with pocket tens, which is a very strong hand in a 3-handed game. Flashlight has raised from the button to 300. To maximize value and prevent the big blind (Pro) from realizing equity with a wide range of hands, I will put in a 3-bet. A raise to 1000 is a standard size that puts pressure on the initial raiser and likely forces the big blind to fold.

I hold A5o in the big blind facing a button open and a 3-bet from the small blind. This hand is too weak to call or 4-bet out of position against a 3-bet, especially with the original raiser still left to act. The clear decision is to fold.

Ace-Queen offsuit is a premium hand in a three-handed game. While Flash has shown aggression with a 3-bet, I am in position and the pot odds (700 to call a 1400 total pot) are favorable. Folding is too tight, and 4-betting would commit too much of my remaining stack, potentially forcing me into an all-in situation when I am only slightly ahead of his range. Calling allows me to see a flop in position and evaluate the board state.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet to 1000. The flop comes Queen-high, which hits a significant portion of a 3-bet calling range (hands like AQ, KQ, QJs). My pocket tens are now an overpair to the board but vulnerable to the Queen. With an SPR of approximately 2 relative to Flashlight's remaining stack, I want to control the pot size. Checking allows me to see how Flashlight reacts; if he checks behind, my tens are likely the best hand, and if he bets, I can evaluate based on his sizing. This dry board doesn't offer many draws, so I don't need to bet for protection immediately.

Flash checked the flop after 3-betting pre-flop. With Top Pair (Queens) and an Ace kicker on a dry board (Qc-6s-2h), my hand is strong enough to bet for value and protection. Checking back could allow hands like King-high or middle pairs to catch a miracle card or simply result in losing value from hands Flash might call with. I will bet 800 (approximately 38% of the pot) to gain information and build the pot while in position.

I am in a 3-bet pot with pocket tens as the pre-flop aggressor. The flop is Queen-high and very dry (Qc-6s-2h). While the Queen is an overcard to my pair, the board is otherwise disconnected. I checked to control the pot size and Flashlight bet 800 into a pot of 2100. This bet is roughly 38% of the pot, offering me good odds to call. Flashlight's range for calling a 3-bet and betting this flop includes some Queens (AQ, KQ, QJs), but also lower pocket pairs (77-99) and potentially some air or backdoors. Folding here would be too tight given the strength of my hand and the pot odds. I will call to see how the turn develops.

On the turn 5h, the board remains relatively dry, though some backdoor heart draws have appeared. My pocket tens are still in a 'way ahead or way behind' situation against Flashlight's range. If Flashlight has a Queen (AQ, KQ, QJs), I am drawing to two outs. If Flashlight has a lower pair (77-99) or is bluffing with high cards, I am ahead. By checking, I continue to control the pot size and allow Flashlight to potentially bet with a wider, weaker range than the one that would call a lead from me. Given Flashlight's remaining stack of 4220 and a pot of 3700, any bet from him will likely be a significant commitment or a shove. I will check to see his next move.

With top pair, Ace kicker on a board of Qc-6s-2h-5h, my hand is likely strong, but the turn card adds potential straight and flush draws. Flash checked, signaling he may be playing passively with a drawing hand or a weaker pair. Checking behind is the most prudent play to control the pot size and realize showdown value, while preventing myself from being forced into a difficult spot if he were to raise a bet. This allows me to see the river and evaluate any further action without unnecessary risk.

The river 4h completes a possible straight with any 3, but that is unlikely given the pre-flop 3-bet. The turn check-check suggests Flashlight likely doesn't have a strong Queen, as he would usually bet for value or protection on such a dry board after betting the flop. My pocket tens have good showdown value against his likely range of medium pairs (77-99) or missed high cards like AJ/AK. Checking is the most prudent move to reach showdown or potentially induce a bluff from missed hands, whereas betting might fold out the hands I beat and only get called by better.

The board has completed a possible straight (3-6) and a flush (any two hearts). My pair of Queens, while strong, is now vulnerable to many hands in Flash's range. Checking behind allows me to see the showdown safely without risking further chips against a potential straight or flush, while still having decent showdown value against missed draws or weaker pairs.

I am on the button with 96o. While I have the chip lead and the tournament is nearing its conclusion (hand 142 of 150), 96o is a very weak hand to open with, even from the button. Both the Small Blind and Big Blind have shown willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands. Given my comfortable lead, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot to apply pressure rather than risking chips in a potential confrontation with the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with an unsuited one-gapper (86o), which is a very weak starting hand. With the Button folding, there's no reason to invest any more chips out of position against the Big Blind. Folding is the optimal play.

Q5o is a weak offsuit hand. Even from the Button, it is generally not profitable to open-raise with a hand this disconnected and weak, especially with two capable players in the blinds. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind and the button (Pro) has folded. With a King-high suited hand, this is a strong position to raise and apply pressure on the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that can effectively steal the blinds or build a pot with a reasonable hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 7-2 suited, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Flashlight raised from the Small Blind to 300. Although the pot odds are 2:1 and the hand is suited, its post-flop playability and raw equity against a standard Small Blind opening range are very poor. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (hand 143 of 150) and I have a comfortable chip lead, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With a weak hand like nine-five offsuit in the button position, I have very little incentive to contest the pot. The hand lacks strength and playability, so folding is the most disciplined and optimal choice to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 7-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Since Flashlight on the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. Given the poor quality of my cards, there is no reason to invest more chips into the pot or attempt a bluff from out of position against a solid player like Pro.

I am on the button with Qc Td, which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. With only 6 hands remaining in the tournament and holding the chip lead, I want to play aggressively from the button to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300.

Facing a 3x open from the Button, I have pocket fives in the Small Blind. With effective stacks around 90bb deep, I have plenty of implied odds to set-mine. While 3-betting as a bluff is an option to attack a wide Button opening range, flat calling keeps variance low and allows me to see a flop cheaply with a hand that has clear post-flop playability (hit a set or check/fold). I will call 300.

I am in the big blind with a very weak hand [3s, 5d].
